items.name: The Town Notice Board
items.description: A cork board on a post near the square's eastern maple. Layered with notices — upcoming events, lost and found, requests for help, a handwritten recipe someone's been sharing for years.
items.content_note: Populated from active events and help tasks

items.name: The War Memorial
items.description: A granite slab bearing four names in relief: WILLIAM HARTWELL, JOSEPH FINCH, THOMAS GRANGE, LENA ALDERMAN. Below: 'Of Brindlewick. 1939-1945. They are not gone while they are remembered.'

items.name: The Statue of Alderman Finch
items.description: A life-sized bronze statue of a man in mid-19th century clothing, one hand raised as if indicating something across the lake. The sculptor's name — Helen Mossgrove — is on the base. There is a small brass fitting on the south side of the base.
items.lore_detail: The brass fitting on the south side of the base is a clock winding port. Very small. You'd only notice it if you were looking for it.

items.name: The Library Catalogue
items.description: Both physical card catalogues and digital terminals. The starting point for any research in Brindlewick.

items.name: The Unsigned Watercolor
items.description: A small watercolor painting in a plain frame: a girl of about 13 sitting at the shore of a lake, looking south. The painting is 19th century in style and quality. In the lower right corner, in very small letters: 'M.F.'
items.lore_note: This is one of three places the initials M.F. appear in Brindlewick.

items.name: The Partial Alderman Diary
items.description: A partial transcription (not the original) of pages from an Alderman family diary, covering 1848-1863. Several pages are missing or illegible. It mentions 'the lake' frequently, and twice mentions 'M.' with particular warmth before the name disappears from subsequent entries.
items.readable_content: ...the lake in evening is extraordinary, as M. always says. She has an eye for light that I did not give her, and I am grateful it came from somewhere... [several pages missing] ...1855, October: M.'s painting of the eastern shore is the finest thing in the study. She should show it to someone. She says no one would want it... [gap] ...1863: M. is Mrs. Alderman now, Thomas's wife. She seems happy. I am glad. The record may not remember her as it should but I will...

items.name: The Covered Bridge Inscription
items.description: On the main upper beam of the covered bridge, in small careful letters requiring you to look up and lean close: 'M.F. was here — 1833. The lake is hers.'
items.lore_note: Second of three M.F. appearances. The year 1833 would make M.F. about 18 — old enough to carve something that meant leaving a mark.

items.name: Mira Finch's Gravestone
items.description: A small plain stone at the back of the cemetery, set slightly apart. 'Mira Finch, 1815-1878. She named the lake.' The stone is relatively new — the lettering is sharp, not worn. Someone placed it recently.
items.lore_note: Third M.F. appearance. This stone wasn't here in the 1960s — someone placed it after doing the research. Someone who has been protecting this story.

items.name: Alderman Finch's Diary
items.description: A leather-bound journal covering 1824-1865. The first entry describes arriving at the lake. The entries are meticulous and warm. Near the end, multiple entries address 'Mira' directly — a letter within the diary that was never sent.

items.name: The Framed Letter
items.description: A framed piece of yellowed paper on Agnes Perkins's kitchen wall. The letter reads: 'Dearest M., I have thought of nothing but the orchard path since Tuesday. Yours, in hope, W.A.' Addressed to 'M. Alderman.' Someone has written at the bottom in newer ink: 'Delivered 1891 to M. PERKINS by mistake. Source of 135 years of theater. — A.P.'

items.name: Mari's Recipe Card
items.description: A handwritten recipe card, the paper slightly browned. The handwriting is beautiful. The third ingredient reads: 'h___y from [ink smudge]___perkins?' The rest is legible.

items.name: Perkins Alpine Honey
items.description: A small jar of amber honey with a handwritten label: 'Perkins Alpine — not for sale, please ask.' It has a distinct floral quality — herbal and sweet, with something underneath that's hard to name.

items.name: The Station Master's Key
items.description: A brass door key on a faded red ribbon, on a nail beside the door of the old Station Master's House. Slightly greened with age. The ribbon has been here since 1923. The key still turns in the lock — the house has never been properly sealed.
items.lore_note: Gerald Hobbs left in November 1923. He expected to return. He never did. The key stayed.

items.name: The Trail Register
items.description: A notebook in a weatherproof box at the trailhead. Handwritten entries from every hiker who's signed in. The earliest entry is 1978. Some entries are brief; some are long. A few are memorable.

notable_entries.text: First entry. April 12. Snow on the Lookout still. Beautiful.
Found what I was looking for. Will return.
My grandmother brought me here when I was 6. She's gone now. The view is exactly the same.

items.name: The Warming Hut Log
items.description: A spiral notebook inside the warming hut, starting from 1985. Shorter entries than the main trail register — most people write when they're cold and glad to be warm.

notable_entries.text: First entry. Hut built last week. Already needed. — Clem Rourke
Found what I was looking for. Will return. (Same as the other book. The same handwriting.)
Snowed in here for 3 hours with a stranger. We talked about everything. Never got their name.

items.name: The Clockwork Inscription
items.description: On the main gear housing of the Town Hall clock, scratched in careful letters almost too small to read without leaning close: 'C.W. 1856 — for T.A. — 3 minutes to be kind.'

items.name: Almanac, 1879
items.description: A general almanac from 1879, water-stained at the edges but readable. The spring flood season is annotated by a previous owner: 'Lake very high. Ice on the creek until May 12. The beacon lost.'

items.name: Historical Photograph Collection
items.description: A timeline of Brindlewick photographs from the 1890s forward. Most are labeled; some aren't. One 1897 photograph shows two people standing at the lakefront in front of what appears to be a tall iron post — the lake beacon, before the 1879 flood would have... wait.
items.lore_note: The photo is labeled 1897. The beacon was supposed to have been lost in 1879. Either the date is wrong, or the beacon was temporarily recovered and re-erected.

items.name: The 1832 Valley Map
items.description: A hand-drawn map of the Brindlewick valley from 1832. Detailed for its time. The lake is labeled 'MIRROWELL LAKE' in clear letters. In the corner, a small compass rose — and in the artist's note at the bottom: 'Named for M. Finch, by her father's account.'

items.name: The Lighthouse Keeper's Log (Copy)
items.description: A photocopy of the original lighthouse log (the original is at the library). The last handwritten entry, November 14, 1957: 'Automating today. 75 years of keeping the light. The lake is peaceful. I hope it stays so.' Signed: E. Webb.
items.lore_note: E. Webb — a Webb, keeping the lake light. The Webb family has been watching over Brindlewick's mechanisms for a long time.

items.name: The Boathouse Key
items.description: A large iron key on a leather fob, worn smooth from handling. Constance Alderman gave it to you. It opens the old Alderman boathouse at the south end of the lake.

items.name: An Anonymous Note
items.description: A small folded note on cream-colored paper, the handwriting precise and careful: 'You are doing better than you know. Keep going. — A Friend.' It was left under your inn room door.

items.name: The Inn Guest Book
items.description: A leather guest book going back to 1903. Teddy keeps it behind the front desk and lets guests read it. Most entries are brief. A 1967 entry reads: 'Found what I was looking for. Will return.' No name.
items.lore_note: The same phrase appears in the trail register (1978 copy) and the warming hut log (2003 copy). Three different handwritings, same phrase, across 36 years.
